annotations.label: Squaring the circle

anchor.snippet: the challenge of constructing a square with the area of a given circle

annotations.note: Mathlib has no theory of compass-and-straightedge geometric constructions, so this construction problem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Impossibility of squaring the circle

anchor.snippet: the task was proven to be impossible, as a consequence of the Lindemann–Weierstrass theorem

annotations.note: Requires both transcendence of π and a constructibility theory, neither of which is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Existence of approximate constructions

anchor.snippet: Approximate constructions with any given non-perfect accuracy exist

annotations.note: No formalization of straightedge-and-compass constructions or their approximation accuracy exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quadrature of the circle

anchor.snippet: The term quadrature of the circle is sometimes used as a synonym for squaring the circle

annotations.note: This is a terminological synonym for the unformalized squaring-the-circle problem.

annotations.label: Babylonian and Egyptian approximations to π

anchor.snippet: the Babylonian mathematicians used the approximation

annotations.note: Mathlib has numerical π bounds but no theorem encoding these specific historical approximations.

annotations.label: Old Testament approximation to π

anchor.snippet: the Old Testament Books of Kings used the simpler approximation

annotations.note: The approximation π ≈ 3 is not formalized as a statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ancient Indian approximations to π

anchor.snippet: used several different approximations to

annotations.note: These specific historical approximations to π are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Archimedes' area of a circle

anchor.snippet: Archimedes proved a formula for the area of a circle

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace.volume_ball_fin_two` states the area of a 2D ball equals r²·π, the special case of the general n-ball volume.

annotations.label: Zu Chongzhi's Milü approximation

anchor.snippet: an approximation known as Milü

annotations.note: The approximation 355/113 to π is not recorded as a named statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Squaring an arbitrary polygon

anchor.snippet: constructions to convert any polygon into a square of equivalent area

annotations.note: Mathlib has no theory of straightedge-and-compass polygon constructions.

annotations.label: Lune of Hippocrates

anchor.snippet: the lune of Hippocrates , that could be squared

annotations.note: The squarable lune of Hippocrates is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Method of exhaustion (Antiphon)

anchor.snippet: inscribing regular polygons within a circle and doubling the number of sides

annotations.note: The classical method of exhaustion is not formalized as such, though limits of inscribed polygons exist in spirit elsewhere.

annotations.label: Bryson's intermediate value argument

anchor.snippet: this principle can be seen as a form of the modern intermediate value theorem

annotations.note: The modern intermediate value theorem cited is fully formalized as `intermediate_value_Icc`.

annotations.label: Quadrature of the hyperbola

anchor.snippet: de Saint-Vincent succeeded in his quadrature of the hyperbola

annotations.note: `integral_inv` proves ∫ₐᵇ x⁻¹ = log(b/a), i.e. the area under the hyperbola is logarithmic.

annotations.label: Irrationality of π (Lambert)

anchor.snippet: Johann Heinrich Lambert proved in 1761 that

annotations.note: `irrational_pi` states `Irrational π`, exactly Lambert's result.

annotations.label: Transcendence of π (Lindemann)

anchor.snippet: Ferdinand von Lindemann succeeded in proving more strongly that

annotations.note: Only the analytic part of the Lindemann–Weierstrass proof exists; transcendence of π itself is not yet proven in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Doubling the cube and angle trisection

anchor.snippet: these cannot be solved by compass and straightedge

annotations.note: Mathlib has no constructibility theory, so the impossibility of doubling the cube or trisecting angles is not formalized.

annotations.label: Reduction to constructing √π

anchor.snippet: the length of the side of a square whose area equals that of a unit circle

annotations.note: This reduction depends on a notion of constructible length, which Mathlib does not formalize.

anchors.snippet: the length of the side of a square whose area equals that of a unit circle

annotations.label: √π constructible implies π constructible

anchor.snippet: it would follow from standard compass and straightedge constructions that

annotations.note: Closure of constructible numbers under squaring is not formalized, as constructibility itself is absent.

anchor.snippet: Pierre Wantzel showed that lengths that could be constructed with compass and straightedge

annotations.note: Wantzel's characterization of constructible lengths (degree a power of 2) is not present in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: constructible lengths must be algebraic numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib has `IsAlgebraic` but no notion of constructible length to relate it to.

annotations.note: Transcendence of π is not yet proven; only the analytic groundwork of the Lindemann–Weierstrass theorem is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Transcendence of e (Hermite)

anchor.snippet: the proof of transcendence of Euler's number

annotations.note: There is no `transcendental_e` theorem; only related analytic integral lemmas toward Lindemann–Weierstrass exist.

anchors.snippet: the proof of transcendence of Euler's number

annotations.label: Rational power of a transcendental is transcendental

anchor.snippet: a rational power of a transcendental number remains transcendental

annotations.note: `Transcendental.pow` proves rⁿ is transcendental for natural n>0, the special case of the more general rational-power claim.

annotations.label: Lindemann–Weierstrass theorem

anchor.snippet: the Lindemann–Weierstrass theorem on linear independence of algebraic powers of

annotations.label: Dinostratus' theorem

anchor.snippet: Dinostratus' theorem uses the quadratrix of Hippias to square the circle

annotations.note: Neither the quadratrix of Hippias nor Dinostratus' theorem appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Archimedean spiral construction

anchor.snippet: The Archimedean spiral can be used for another similar construction

annotations.note: Squaring the circle via the Archimedean spiral is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Neusis cannot square the circle

anchor.snippet: neusis construction cannot square the circle

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of neusis constructions or their limitations.

annotations.label: Squaring the circle in hyperbolic geometry

anchor.snippet: it sometimes can be in hyperbolic geometry under suitable interpretations of the terms

annotations.note: Constructions in the hyperbolic plane and their areas are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regular quadrilaterals in the hyperbolic plane

anchor.snippet: but instead it contains regular quadrilaterals

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize regular quadrilaterals or regular polygons in the hyperbolic plane.

annotations.label: Constructible equal-area pairs in hyperbolic plane

anchor.snippet: infinitely many pairs of constructible circles and constructible regular quadrilaterals of equal area

annotations.note: No hyperbolic constructibility or equal-area results of this kind exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rational approximations are constructible

anchor.snippet: It takes only elementary geometry to convert any given rational approximation of

annotations.note: Constructibility of rational lengths is not formalized, as Mathlib lacks geometric constructions.
